banner: Week 6 · Parallel, Perpendicular & the Perfect Half
essential_question: Two straight lines share a plane forever — what are the only two ways they can relate, and what does it take to cut an angle or a segment exactly in half?
objective: Distinguish parallel lines from perpendicular lines using ∥ and ⊥, define bisector, perpendicular bisector, and midpoint, and use a straightedge and compass to construct a perpendicular bisector and bisect an angle.

content.prompt: Lay two straightedges so they never touch, no matter how far you slide them — that's one relationship. Now cross them so the corner looks exactly like a picture frame's corner — that's the other. Geometry says these are the ONLY two special relationships two lines in one plane can have. What's different about the two crossings you just made?

agenda.title: I do — Parallel, Perpendicular, Bisector & Midpoint

content.heading: Two lines, two relationships — and the tool that cuts things in half
bullets: Parallel lines (ℓ ∥ m) share one plane and never intersect — they stay the same distance apart forever.
Perpendicular lines (ℓ ⊥ m) intersect and form a right angle (90°) at the point where they cross.
A bisector is a line, ray, or segment that cuts an angle or a segment into two congruent (equal) parts.
A perpendicular bisector is a bisector of a segment that ALSO crosses it at a right angle — cutting it in half AND meeting it at 90°.
The midpoint is the one point that splits a segment into two congruent halves: if AB ≅ BD, then B is the midpoint of AD.

content.heading: Move the endpoints; watch the bisector hold the middle
content.note: On the sim, drag either endpoint P or Q. Watch the midpoint M and the perpendicular bisector update live. Confirm the readout PM = MQ stays true no matter how you stretch or tilt the segment.

content.preview: Build session: construct a perpendicular bisector and an angle bisector with a real compass and straightedge.

success_criteria.text: I can tell parallel and perpendicular lines apart and use the ∥ and ⊥ symbols correctly
I can define bisector, perpendicular bisector, and midpoint, and find a midpoint from congruent segments
I can use a straightedge and compass to construct a segment's perpendicular bisector and bisect an angle

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W06 Parallel, Perpendicular & Bisectors.md

questions.prompt: Two lines in the same plane that never intersect are ___ lines.
questions.explain: Parallel lines stay in the same plane and the same distance apart forever — they never cross.

questions.prompt: What does the notation ℓ ⊥ m mean?
options: ℓ and m intersect and form a right angle
ℓ and m are the same line
questions.answer: ℓ and m intersect and form a right angle
questions.explain: The ⊥ symbol means 'is perpendicular to' — the two lines cross and form a 90° angle there.

questions.prompt: In your own words, define a bisector. What can it cut, and into what?
questions.answer: A bisector is a line, ray, or segment that cuts an angle or a segment into two congruent (equal) parts.
questions.explain: Full credit names 'two congruent/equal parts' and that a bisector can apply to either an angle or a segment.

questions.prompt: Using only a straightedge and compass — no ruler measuring — construct the perpendicular bisector of segment CD on the grid, and label the point where it crosses CD as M.
questions.answer: Equal-radius arcs swung from C and from D (radius more than half of CD) crossing above and below the segment; a straight line through both arc-crossings; that line meets CD at its midpoint, labeled M.
questions.explain: Rubric: two equal-radius arcs from each endpoint crossing above and below the segment (1 pt) + the connecting line correctly meeting CD at its midpoint M (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Points A, B, D lie on a line in that order. If AB ≅ BD, then B is the ___ of AD.
questions.explain: B splits AD into two congruent segments, AB and BD — that's exactly what makes B the midpoint.

questions.prompt: Infinitely many lines pass through a segment's midpoint, and every one of them bisects the segment. What one extra condition makes just ONE of those lines the perpendicular bisector?
questions.answer: It must also cross the segment at exactly a right angle (90°) — at any other angle it's just 'a bisector,' not 'the perpendicular bisector.'
questions.explain: Full credit names the 90° crossing as the one extra condition that upgrades an ordinary bisector of a segment to its perpendicular bisector.

questions.prompt: Exit: which is harder to spot in the real world — a true perpendicular (crossing at exactly 90°) or a true midpoint (splitting something exactly in half)? Give one real example of whichever you picked, and say how you'd check it without a ruler or protractor.
